critic_raw:
This design fine-tunes a clinical transformer to predict depression severity (PHQ-9) from EHR data versus ridge regression on demographic/clinical features.

The label is defined as 'the most recently recorded patient-reported depression severity score within 30 days of a clinical encounter.' This means the label is a contemporaneous patient report, not a future outcome. The transformer's clinical notes (all encounter notes in the 12 months prior to the prediction date) will include language that correlates with mood state at the time of note writing — descriptions of emotional symptoms, affect documentation, medication adherence notes. A well-trained language model on these notes is not predicting depression severity; it is extracting depression-related language that was documented contemporaneously with the PHQ-9 score. This is near-circular: the note documents the same clinical state that the PHQ-9 measures. The baseline ridge regression on demographics and comorbidities lacks this contemporaneous signal, creating a structural information asymmetry that is not an architectural advantage.

The design uses Clinical BERT fine-tuned end-to-end. Fine-tuning a full transformer on 36 months of single-system EHR data risks domain-specific overfitting: the model will adapt to the idiosyncratic documentation practices of this particular health system, and the test set performance (from months 49–60 of the same system) will reflect system-level consistency rather than genuine clinical pattern generalization. The temporal split partially mitigates this, but documentation practices are slow-moving; overfitting to system style will persist across the 5-year window.

Patient-level temporal integrity is enforced by assigning all records for a single patient to one split. This is correct for preventing within-patient leakage. However, the design also states that all encounter notes in the 12 months prior to the prediction date are used as input. For patients near split boundaries, notes from the 12-month lookback window could include notes from the training period if the prediction date falls in the validation window but the lookback extends before month 37. This is a form of temporal leakage that the design does not address.
all_issues_raised: The transformer's clinical notes from the 12 months prior to the prediction date will contain contemporaneous mood documentation, making the prediction task near-circular — the notes reflect the same clinical state as the PHQ-9 label rather than predicting a future state.

Fine-tuning Clinical BERT end-to-end on a single health system's records will adapt to that system's idiosyncratic documentation style; test set performance from the same system's later period reflects style consistency, not clinical generalization.

For patients with prediction dates early in the validation window (month 37), a 12-month lookback extends into the training period, potentially including notes that correlate with training-period label distributions — a form of temporal leakage the design does not address.
all_issues_adjudicated: The transformer's clinical notes from the 12 months prior to the prediction date will contain contemporaneous mood documentation, making the prediction task near-circular — the notes reflect the same clinical state as the PHQ-9 label rather than predicting a future state.

Fine-tuning Clinical BERT end-to-end on a single health system's records will adapt to that system's idiosyncratic documentation style; test set performance from the same system's later period reflects style consistency, not clinical generalization.

For patients with prediction dates early in the validation window (month 37), a 12-month lookback extends into the training period, potentially including notes that correlate with training-period label distributions — a form of temporal leakage the design does not address.
